./docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md:196:**Integration tests: zero mocks.** No exceptions. Integration tests exercise real production implementations of every dependency in the graph — real git (`t.TempDir()` + scrubbed `GIT_*` env), real filesystem, real bead store, real coordinator, real server, real agent harness (the `script` harness below is the only approved fake for the AI-provider boundary, and it does not mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically). A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.
./docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md:209:**The only approved fake in the DDx test suite is the `script` harness.** It is the *AI-provider* boundary replacement, and it is not a mock — it is a deterministic alternate implementation of the `agent.Runner` interface that reads a line-based directive file and performs real filesystem + git operations against the worktree. The `virtual` harness is a replay-only fake retained for prompt-routing unit tests; it does not write files or make commits.

./cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/bead-jsonl-oversized-line.jsonl:1:{"acceptance":"- [ ] JWT middleware installed in data-plane router as optional layer (runs when Authorization header present)\n- [ ] JwtIssuer constructed at startup in serve.rs (dev: random key with warning)\n- [ ] AuditAttribution populated on every audit write when ResolvedIdentity is present\n- [ ] gRPC service.rs migrated from x-axon-database to composite x-axon-tenant-database\n- [ ] gRPC service.rs gained optional JWT verify path\n- [ ] cutover_jwt_test.rs integration test with real JWT end-to-end\n- [ ] scripts/cutover-smoke.sh script runs end-to-end against dev server\n- [ ] cargo test --workspace green\n- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean\n- [ ] Legacy auth paths (--no-auth, --guest-role, Tailscale) still work unchanged","closing_commit_sha":"1a378fe785612ab9a24241977cf60c0c13e150b9","created_at":"2026-04-15T11:54:30.489788638Z","description":"Finish wiring the ADR-018 auth stack into the real request pipeline. After the smoke test on commit e83cf02, D3 already landed more than expected: `resolve_tenant_handler` composes `{tenant}:{database}` into the TenantRouter slug, giving real per-pair physical isolation. What remains is the three things that are *built but not installed*.\n\n## What is already live (confirmed by smoke test)\n\n- Path-based routing: /tenants/:t/databases/:d/... routed to per-pair SQLite files via composite slug\n- GraphQL under the nested path\n- Unprefixed REST + /graphql return 404\n- HttpAxonClient in the SDK + the UI use the new paths\n- Legacy X-Axon-Database header is gone from REST\n\n## What is still NOT installed (the gap this bead closes)\n\n### 1. JWT pipeline not enforced\n\nB1 landed `auth_pipeline::jwt_verify_layer` + `JwtIssuer`. The real gateway router in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` does NOT install this layer. The server currently runs in legacy auth mode (Tailscale or --no-auth). Install the layer as an OPTIONAL middleware: if the request has an Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline; if not, fall back to legacy auth.\n\nWire point: in `build_router_with_auth` (or wherever the data-plane nested router is constructed), add `.layer(from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), jwt_verify_layer))` before the tenant_handler middleware. The layer needs access to a `JwtIssuer` and the `StorageAdapter`. Add them to the AppState struct.\n\nCreate the JwtIssuer at server startup in `serve.rs`. Read the signing key from an env var `AXON_JWT_KEY` (base64 HMAC secret); if unset, generate a random 32-byte key at startup and log a warning (dev mode). Production key management is a future concern.\n\nThe legacy auth path (crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs Identity + Role) still works for requests without an Authorization header — the JWT layer is a no-op pass-through when the header is absent. This preserves --no-auth, --guest-role, and Tailscale whois behavior exactly as today.\n\n### 2. Audit attribution not populated\n\nF1 added `AuditAttribution` as an optional field on `AuditEntry` plus the `with_attribution()` builder. No handler currently calls it. In the JWT middleware (step 1), after `ResolvedIdentity` is installed into the request extension, also expose a `current_attribution()` helper on AppState that the audit write sites can call.\n\nFind every `AuditEntry::new(...)` call site in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` (there are probably 10-20) and chain `.with_attribution(current_attribution(\u0026request))` when a ResolvedIdentity is present. When absent (legacy auth), leave the attribution as None — the existing `actor: String` field still works.\n\n### 3. gRPC service.rs still reads x-axon-database\n\n`crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:26` has `const AXON_DATABASE_HEADER: \u0026str = \"x-axon-database\"` and `grpc_current_database` reads it from metadata. The gRPC service is entirely separate from the REST gateway and D3 did not touch it. Replace this header read with path-based extraction... except gRPC doesn't have URL paths in the same way. gRPC calls use service + method names, not paths. The database has to come from either:\n\n(a) A metadata field (still a header-equivalent), OR\n(b) A new field on every request proto\n\n(a) is pragmatic. Rename the metadata header from `x-axon-database` to `x-axon-tenant-database` and carry a composite value `\"{tenant}:{database}\"`. This matches the REST composite slug. The gRPC service parses it and dispatches via the same `get_or_create_any` path.\n\nAlso add `x-axon-authorization` (or reuse the standard `authorization` metadata key) for JWT credentials. Run the JWT verify pipeline in the gRPC `check_auth` step before dispatching to the handler.\n\nFor this bead, the gRPC change is small:\n- Rename the metadata constant\n- Update `grpc_current_database` to parse the composite form\n- Update the Rust + TS gRPC clients to send the composite form\n- Add JWT auth path in `check_auth`\n\n### 4. DatabaseRouter NOT swapped in (deferred — not needed right now)\n\nD2 landed `DatabaseRouter` as a new primitive, but the gateway still uses `TenantRouter::get_or_create_any(slug)`. The smoke test confirmed this composite slug works correctly. Replacing TenantRouter with DatabaseRouter is a refactor that doesn't change behavior — defer it to a later cleanup bead. Not in this bead's scope.\n\n## Scope summary\n\nThis bead does exactly three things:\n\n1. Install B1's JWT middleware in the data-plane router, optional path (Authorization header present)\n2. Populate F1's AuditAttribution at every audit write site when ResolvedIdentity is present\n3. Migrate gRPC service.rs from x-axon-database header to the composite slug + optional JWT metadata\n\n## Tests for this bead\n\n### Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs (NEW)\n\n- create a JwtIssuer with a known key\n- mint a JWT for a default user with grants on (default, default)\n- start the in-process test server harness that gateway_test.rs uses\n- send a request with Authorization: Bearer \u003cjwt\u003e to /tenants/default/databases/default/collections\n- assert 200 (the JWT pipeline accepted + the handler saw ResolvedIdentity)\n\n### Integration test: attribution populated\n\n- send an entity-create request with a JWT\n- query the audit log\n- assert the audit entry has `attribution.user_id` set to the JWT sub\n\n### gRPC test: composite slug\n\n- send a CreateEntity gRPC request with metadata `x-axon-tenant-database: acme:orders`\n- assert routing to the acme:orders database (check the storage file exists)\n\n### Manual smoke test\n\nStart the server, issue a JWT via `/control/tenants/default/credentials`, use the HttpAxonClient with the JWT, create an entity, verify the audit log has attribution. Write a short shell script under `scripts/cutover-smoke.sh` that does this end-to-end.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- DatabaseRouter replacement — deferred\n- OIDC federation — follow-up\n- Signing key rotation — future ADR\n- Removing legacy Identity/Role types from auth.rs — they still serve the --no-auth and Tailscale paths\n\n## Files to touch\n\n- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — install JWT middleware, thread AuditAttribution into audit writes (10-20 sites)\n- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — construct JwtIssuer at startup\n- crates/axon-server/src/service.rs — rename header constant, parse composite, add JWT path\n- crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs — NEW\n- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh — NEW\n- Possibly sdk/typescript/src/client.ts — update gRPC metadata key if touching gRPC SDK surface\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n\n- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, just consume it\n- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope for removal\n- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes\n- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes\n\n## Verification\n\n- cargo test -p axon-server (all prior tests green + new cutover_jwt_test)\n- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean\n- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh runs end-to-end against a dev ./cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/reviewer-stream-oversized-body.jsonl:1:{"id":"as-bc4b0e90","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:50:16.151938804Z","harness":"claude","surface":"claude","canonical_target":"claude-sonnet-4-6","model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","prompt_len":13868,"prompt":"\u003cexecute-bead\u003e\n  \u003cbead id=\"axon-dccbdf73\"\u003e\n    \u003ctitle\u003eauth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension\u003c/title\u003e\n    \u003cdescription\u003e\nImplement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server.\n\n## What you're building\n\nAn axum middleware layer that runs on every data-plane request. It validates the JWT, installs a (user_id, tenant_id, grants) tuple into the request extension, and returns a typed AuthError on any failure. Downstream handlers read the extension and trust it completely.\n\n## Scope — exactly what to add\n\n### New file: `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`\n\nA module exposing two things:\n\n1. A `JwtIssuer` helper that signs a `JwtClaims` into a compact JWT string. HS256 is the only algorithm required for this bead. The signing key is a `Vec\u003cu8\u003e` passed in at construction. Provide `issue(\u0026self, claims: \u0026JwtClaims) -\u003e Result\u003cString, AuthError\u003e` and a `verify(\u0026self, token: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e` for round-tripping in tests.\n\n2. An axum middleware `async fn jwt_verify_layer` that runs the 8-step verification order below. On success, installs a `ResolvedIdentity { user_id, tenant_id, grants }` into the request extension. On failure, returns a response built via `AuthError::into_response()` so the mapping to HTTP status + error.code is consistent.\n\nThe middleware also owns an `InMemoryRevocationCache` (a small `tokio::sync::RwLock\u003cHashSet\u003cUuid\u003e\u003e` is fine; nothing fancy) and falls back to SQL (via the storage adapter's new `is_jti_revoked` method) on cache miss.\n\n### Extend: `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs`\n\n- Add `ResolvedIdentity { pub user_id: UserId, pub tenant_id: TenantId, pub grants: Grants }`.\n- Add `impl AuthError` with `pub fn status_code(\u0026self) -\u003e u16` returning 401 or 403 per ADR-018 §4 failure table, and `pub fn error_code(\u0026self) -\u003e \u0026'static str` returning the stable code string. (An axum `IntoResponse` impl can live in axon-server.)\n- Add `impl Grants` with `pub fn find_database(\u0026self, name: \u0026str) -\u003e Option\u003c\u0026GrantedDatabase\u003e` and `impl GrantedDatabase` with `pub fn has_op(\u0026self, op: Op) -\u003e bool`.\n\n### Extend: `crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs` (trait)\n\nAdd one method to the `StorageAdapter` trait:\n\n```rust\nasync fn is_jti_revoked(\u0026self, jti: Uuid) -\u003e Result\u003cbool, AxonError\u003e;\n```\n\nImplement it in `crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs` (store a `HashSet\u003cUuid\u003e`; default-empty), `crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs`, and `crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs` (`SELECT 1 FROM credential_revocations WHERE jti = $1`). The A1 schema (`auth_schema.rs`) already has the `credential_revocations` table — just query it.\n\n### Wire into: `crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs`\n\nRe-export `auth_pipeline::{jwt_verify_layer, JwtIssuer, InMemoryRevocationCache}`. Don't wire it into the main router yet — that's D-phase work. For this bead, the middleware just needs to exist and be testable in isolation.\n\n## Verification order (normative — ADR-018 §4)\n\n1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer \u003cjwt\u003e` from the request. Missing header → `AuthError::Unauthenticated` (401). The `--no-auth` bypass is NOT part of this bead — that's E2.\n2. Parse and verify the JWT signature with HS256 against the issuer's key. Signature invalid → `CredentialInvalid` (401). Malformed structure → `CredentialMalformed` (401). `aud` as a JSON array → `CredentialMalformed` (this falls out of the A2 `parse_claims` wrapper — use it).\n3. Check `nbf` and `exp` against `SystemTime::now()` with a 30s skew budget. `exp` more than 30s in the past → `CredentialExpired` (401). `nbf` more than 30s in the future → `CredentialNotYetValid` (401).\n4. Check `claims.jti` against the revocation cache, then SQL on miss. Hit → `CredentialRevoked` (401). Populate the cache on SQL hit to speed up subsequent checks.\n5. Compare `claims.aud` to the `{tenant}` segment extracted from the URL path. Mismatch → `CredentialWrongTenant` (**403**, not 401 — see the table). The URL extraction path is: look at `request.uri().path()` and parse the leading `/tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/...` segments. For this bead, implement a small helper `fn extract_tenant_database(path: \u0026str) -\u003e Option\u003c(\u0026str, \u0026str)\u003e` that returns `None` for non-data-plane paths so they skip the middleware.\n6. Look up the `sub` UUID in the `users` table via a new `StorageAdapter::get_user(UserId) -\u003e Result\u003cOption\u003cUser\u003e, AxonError\u003e` method. User not found or `user.suspended_at_ms.is_some()` → `UserSuspended` (401).\n7. Check tenant membership: a new `StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member(TenantId, UserId) -\u003e Result\u003cOption\u003cTenantMember\u003e, AxonError\u003e`. Missing → `NotATenantMember` (403).\n8. Walk `claims.grants.databases[]` looking for an entry matching the URL `{database}`. None → `DatabaseNotGranted` (403). Found → compute the required op from the HTTP method (GET = Read, POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE = Write) and intersect with the grant's ops. Empty intersection → `OpNotGranted` (403). Admin-requiring routes are out of scope for this bead — the middleware only handles Read/Write classification.\n9. Build `ResolvedIdentity` and install it via `request.extensions_mut().insert(resolved)`.\n\n## Error mapping — must exactly match ADR-018 §4\n\nThe mapping table is copied here verbatim. Do not invent variants; the 14 AuthError variants already exist in axon-core.\n\n| Failure | Status | `error.code` |\n|---|---|---|\n| No Authorization header | 401 | `unauthenticated` |\n| Header not `Bearer \u003ctoken\u003e` or base64 broken | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| `aud` as JSON array | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| Signature invalid | 401 | `credential_invalid` |\n| `exp` in past (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_expired` |\n| `nbf` in future (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_not_yet_valid` |\n| `jti` revoked | 401 | `credential_revoked` |\n| `iss` unknown | 401 | `credential_foreign_issuer` — for this bead, reject if `claims.iss != issuer.issuer_id` |\n| `aud` ≠ URL tenant | **403** | `credential_wrong_tenant` |\n| `sub` suspended/deleted | 401 | `user_suspended` |\n| `sub` not a tenant member | **403** | `not_a_tenant_member` |\n| URL database not in grants | **403** | `database_not_granted` |\n| Op not in grant | **403** | `op_not_granted` |\n\nNote the 401 vs 403 split: credential-level problems are 401, scope-level problems are 403.\n\n## Testing — all must pass for this bead to be complete\n\n### Unit tests in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs`\n\nFor every row of the error mapping table, build the minimal defective request and assert the middleware produces exactly that `(status, error_code)` pair. Table-driven with `#[test]` or a single `#[test]` that iterates an array of cases.\n\nAdditionally:\n\n- `jwt_roundtrip_succeeds`: issue a valid JWT with `JwtIssuer::issue`, verify it with `JwtIssuer::verify`, assert claims match.\n- `valid_jwt_populates_request_extension`: run the full middleware on a valid JWT, assert the response is 200 and the handler sees `ResolvedIdentity` in extensions.\n- `revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second`: issue a valid JWT, verify it succeeds, insert the jti into the revocation cache, verify the next request returns 401 `credential_revoked`. Do NOT use sleep — invalidate the cache directly.\n- `clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected`: construct a JWT with `exp` at `now - 29s` (should succeed) and one with `exp` at `now - 31s` (should fail with `credential_expired`). Do the same for `nbf`.\n- `ops_matrix`: for each HTTP method in {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE} and each op set in the grant, assert the expected 200/403 outcome.\n\n### Integration test in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs`\n\nBuild a minimal axum router with one dummy route `GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping`. Install the middleware. Hit it with:\n- No auth header → 401 `unauthenticated`\n- Valid JWT with `grants: [{name: \"orders\", ops: [read]}]` → 200\n- Valid JWT with the wrong tenant in aud → 403 `credential_wrong_tenant`\n- Valid JWT with grants for a different database → 403 `database_not_granted`\n\n### axon-sim workload `inv_018_grant_enforcement`\n\n**If this is too large for one pass, skip the axon-sim workload and note it in the commit message for a follow-up bead.** The core verification must ship; the simulation workload can follow. Do not block the whole bead on the workload.\n\n## Crate dependencies you will need\n\nAdd to `crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml` under `[dependencies]`:\n```toml\njsonwebtoken = \"9\"\n```\n\nEverything else (axum, tower, tokio, uuid, serde_json, http) is already in the workspace.\n\n## Context you must know about\n\n### A1 landed: schema\n- `crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs` has the SQL for `users`, `user_identities`, `tenant_users`, `credential_revocations`, etc.\n- `user_identities.external_id` (NOT `subject`) — already corrected.\n- Migration runs via `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` / `_postgres`.\n\n### A2 landed: types\n- `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` has all the types: `TenantId`, `UserId`, `User`, `UserIdentity`, `Tenant`, `TenantMember`, `TenantRole`, `Op`, `GrantedDatabase`, `Grants`, `JwtClaims`, `AuthError` (14 variants).\n- `parse_claims(\u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e` wraps serde_json and maps errors to `CredentialMalformed`. USE IT.\n- `AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14` is enforced via compile-time exhaustive match.\n- Delegation helper on `TenantRole`: `admin.can_delegate(op)` / etc.\n\n### Files you should NOT touch\n- Anything outside `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`, `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` (additive), `crates/axon-storage/src/{adapter,memory,sqlite,postgres}.rs` (additive), and the two new test files.\n- The existing `crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs` — that's the Tailscale whois path. Leave it alone. Your pipeline is a new module.\n\n## Quality bar\n\nYou are the implementation step. A post-merge review with opus will verify the result against the AC. Axon server — HTTP gateway, gRPC service, MCP stdio, and authentication.\\n2\\t//!\\n3\\t//! # Authentication\\n4\\t//!\\n5\\t//! Identity is resolved once per request by [`auth::AuthContext`] and\\n6\\t//! injected as a typed extension.  Three modes are supported:\\n7\\t//!\\n8\\t//! | Mode | Flag | Actor | Role |\\n9\\t//! |------|------|-------|------|\\n10\\t//! | `NoAuth` | `--no-auth` | `\\\"anonymous\\\"` | Admin |\\n11\\t//! | `Tailscale` | *(default)* | node name | from ACL tags |\\n12\\t//! | `Guest` | `--guest-role \u003crole\u003e` | `\\\"guest\\\"` | configured role |\\n13\\t//!\\n14\\t//! In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\\n15\\t//! Unix socket (`/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock` by default) and calls\\n16\\t//! `/localapi/v0/whois?addr=\u003cpeer\u003e` to resolve the connecting node's identity.\\n17\\t//! Resolved identities are cached by peer IP for the duration of\\n18\\t//! `--auth-cache-ttl-secs` (default 60 s).\\n19\\t//!\\n20\\t//! ACL tag → role mapping:\\n21\\t//! - `tag:axon-admin` → [`Role::Admin`]\\n22\\t//! - `tag:axon-write` / `tag:axon-agent` → [`Role::Write`]\\n23\\t//! - `tag:axon-read` → [`Role::Read`]\\n24\\t//! - no matching tag → `--tailscale-default-role` (default `read`)\\n25\\t//!\\n26\\t//! Connections that are not on the tailnet are rejected with HTTP 401 / gRPC\\n27\\t//! `UNAUTHENTICATED`. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:52:{"id":"corpus-368f5152b39b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"layer-2 design: should execute-bead workers create beads in-band?","description":"During investigation of ddx-7eab13a6 (bead-id resolver worktree bug), a design question surfaced: is it correct for execute-bead workers to call `ddx bead create` directly in-band?\n\nThree alternatives:\n\n(a) Append to parent bead. Worker appends discovered sub-tasks as structured items in the parent's notes or a new discovered-subtask event. The parent becomes the durable record; future runs or the operator can decompose.\n\n(b) Surface via result. Agent emits a structured discovered_subtasks: [...] field in result.json; the execute-loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.\n\n(c) Create beads in-band (current behavior). Worker calls ddx bead create directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue on over-decomposition.\n\nToday's behavior is (c) but undocumented. One worker spawned 11 read-coverage children in a single run (ddx-44236615), all P0, without operator review. The work may be valid but the pattern needs a documented position.\n\nAdditionally, 22 existing beads have malformed IDs of the form .execute-bead-wt-\u003cparent\u003e-\u003ctimestamp\u003e-\u003crandom\u003e-\u003chex\u003e (caused by the layer-1 bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6). A mass-repair or close+refile decision should be made once the design position is set.\n\nScope: pick (a), (b), or (c) and document in a spec amendment (FEAT-006 or FEAT-010 if applicable); record the rationale. Also decide whether to repair the 22 existing malformed-ID beads.","labels":["area:bead","area:agent","kind:design"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-d3a4f89c","title":"layer-2 design: should execute-bead workers create beads in-band?","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"During investigation of ddx-7eab13a6 (bead-id resolver worktree bug), a design question surfaced: is it correct for execute-bead workers to call `ddx bead create` directly in-band?\n\nThree alternatives:\n\n(a) Append to parent bead. Worker appends discovered sub-tasks as structured items in the parent's notes or a new discovered-subtask event. The parent becomes the durable record; future runs or the operator can decompose.\n\n(b) Surface via result. Agent emits a structured discovered_subtasks: [...] field in result.json; the execute-loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.\n\n(c) Create beads in-band (current behavior). Worker calls ddx bead create directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue on over-decomposition.\n\nToday's behavior is (c) but undocumented. One worker spawned 11 read-coverage children in a single run (ddx-44236615), all P0, without operator review. The work may be valid but the pattern needs a documented position.\n\nAdditionally, 22 existing beads have malformed IDs of the form .execute-bead-wt-\u003cparent\u003e-\u003ctimestamp\u003e-\u003crandom\u003e-\u003chex\u003e (caused by the layer-1 bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6). ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:63:{"id":"corpus-42801b4c2e9b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"agent: hardcoded 15-minute DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout kills thinking-route-target requests; not configurable","description":"Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30\n  const DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute\n\nThis is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every upstream-service regardless of route-target class. It is set on every service path:\n  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  Upstream-serviceTimeout: DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout\n  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           Upstream-serviceTimeout: DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout\n\nThe comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'\n\nBut that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:\n  const DefaultUpstream-serviceIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute\n\nThe 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.\n\nConcrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):\n\n1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)\n   - status: no_changes\n   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)\n   - log: 'WARN upstream-service error, retrying attempt=1 err=\"upstream-service request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s\" delay=1s'\n   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.\n\n2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)\n   - All 3 hit 'upstream-service request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt\n   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each\n\nWhy thinking route-targets hit this:\nqwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning route-targets can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the runner considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the runner is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.\n\nWhy arbitrary timeouts are not helping:\n\na) Same value for cloud upstream-services (claude, codex) and local upstream-services (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking route-targets routinely need \u003e15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.\n\nb) No way to opt out of the cap per upstream-service, per route-target, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.\n\nc) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.\n\nd) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking route-target the timeout doesn't mean 'broken upstream-service', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.","acceptance":"AC1. Upstream-serviceTimeout is configurable per-project via .ddx/config.yaml under agent.routing or agent.endpoints — e.g. agent.endpoints[upstream-service].request_timeout_seconds — and the current 15-minute hardcoded constant becomes a default that can be overridden upward (or removed entirely) per upstream-service.\n\nAC2. Upstream-serviceTimeout is configurable per-tier via the route-target-overrides path: cheap tier on a known thinking route-target (qwen3.6, deepseek-r1, etc.) defaults to a longer cap (e.g. 60 min or unlimited). Cloud chat route-targets keep the 15-min default.\n\nAC3. The idle-read timeout (DefaultUpstream-serviceIdleReadTimeout = 5 min) is the primary stalled-TCP-socket defense; documentation in cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go and the user-facing config docs explain the relationship between idle-read and wall-clock-request timeouts and when each fires.\n\nAC4. When Upstream-serviceTimeout fires, the error message names the exact timeout setting and the path to override it (e.g. 'request_timeout=15m exceeded; configure agent.endpoints.\u003cname\u003e.request_timeout_seconds in .ddx/config.yaml').\n\nAC5. execute-loop / execute-bead --request-timeout DURATION CLI flag exists for one-off overrides during debugging.\n\nAC6. Default for known-thinking-route-target surfaces (upstream-service.type=lmstudio AND route-target name matches qwen3.6/qwen-r1/deepseek-r1 etc., or some other declared 'thinking' attribute) is bumped to a sensible value (e.g. 60 min) without requiring per-project config.","labels":["area:agent","area:routing","area:runner","kind:design-flaw"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-2c63bb95","title":"agent: hardcoded 15-minute DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout kills thinking-route-target requests; not configurable","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30\n  const DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute\n\nThis is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every upstream-service regardless of route-target class. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:66:{"id":"corpus-47f5902056ab","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Add project-scoped route middleware and migrate document/bead/graph routes","description":"## Scope\nAdd project-scoping middleware to the DDx server and migrate all document, bead, and doc-graph HTTP routes to canonical project-scoped paths.\n\n## Context\nThe server currently registers ~50 routes under /api/... without project scoping. FEAT-002 requires /api/projects/{project}/... as canonical. Three worker routes already use this pattern (handleProjectWorkerList, handleProjectWorkerShow, handleProjectWorkerProgress) — follow that pattern.\n\n## Implementation approach\n1. Create a projectMiddleware (or helper function) that:\n   - Extracts {project} from the URL path\n   - Resolves it via GetProjectByID() or GetProjectByPath()\n   - Injects the resolved ProjectEntry into request context\n   - Returns 404 if project not found\n2. Register all document, bead, and doc-graph routes under /api/projects/{project}/...\n3. Keep legacy /api/... routes as singleton compatibility aliases that auto-resolve to the single registered project (error if \u003e1 project registered without scoping)\n4. Add test(s) that register two projects, verify scoped routes resolve correctly, and verify legacy routes work with one project\n\n## Files to touch\n- cli/internal/server/server.go — route registration, middleware\n- cli/internal/server/handlers.go (or individual handler files) — extract project from context instead of hardcoding\n- cli/internal/server/server_test.go — new project-scoped routing tests\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n- MCP tools (separate bead)\n- GraphQL schema (already has beadsByProject, workersByProject)\n- Frontend routes","acceptance":"(1) A projectMiddleware or projectResolver extracts {project} from /api/projects/{project}/... routes and injects resolved ProjectEntry into request context. (2) Document routes (list, read, write, search), bead routes (list, show, create, update, claim, unclaim, reopen, ready, blocked, status, deps), and doc graph routes (graph, stale, deps, dependents, history, diff, show, write) are all registered under /api/projects/{project}/... in addition to existing legacy routes. (3) Legacy unscoped routes continue to work when exactly one project is registered (singleton compatibility). (4) cd cli \u0026\u0026 go test ./internal/server -run 'TestProject' -count=1 passes with at least one test covering scoped route resolution.","labels":["phase:build","area:api","kind:implementation"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-36d2775f","title":"Add project-scoped route middleware and migrate document/bead/graph routes","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"## Scope\nAdd project-scoping middleware to the DDx server and migrate all document, bead, and doc-graph HTTP routes to canonical project-scoped paths.\n\n## Context\nThe server currently registers ~50 routes under /api/... without project scoping. FEAT-002 requires /api/projects/{project}/... as canonical. Three worker routes already use this pattern (handleProjectWorkerList, handleProjectWorkerShow, handleProjectWorkerProgress) — follow that pattern.\n\n## Implementation approach\n1. Create a projectMiddleware (or helper function) that:\n   - Extracts {project} from the URL path\n   - Resolves it via GetProjectByID() or GetProjectByPath()\n   - Injects the resolved ProjectEntry into request context\n   - Returns 404 if project not found\n2. Register all document, bead, and doc-graph routes under /api/projects/{project}/...\n3. Keep legacy /api/... routes as singleton compatibility aliases that auto-resolve to the single registered project (error if \u003e1 project registered without scoping)\n4. Add test(s) that register two projects, verify scoped routes resolve correctly, and verify legacy routes work with one project\n\n## Files to touch\n- cli/internal/server/server.go — route registration, middleware\n- cli/internal/server/handlers.go (or individual handler files) — extract project from context instead of hardcoding\n- cli/internal/server/server_test.go — new project-scoped routing tests\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n- MCP tools (separate bead)\n- GraphQL schema (already has beadsByProject, workersByProject)\n- Frontend routes","acceptance":"(1) A projectMiddleware or projectResolver extracts {project} from /api/projects/{project}/... routes and injects resolved ProjectEntry into request context. (2) Document routes (list, read, write, search), bead routes (list, show, create, update, claim, unclaim, reopen, ready, blocked, status, deps), and doc graph routes (graph, stale, deps, dependents, history, diff, show, write) are all registered under /api/projects/{project}/... in addition to existing legacy routes. (3) Legacy unscoped routes continue to work when exactly one project is registered (singleton compatibility). (4) cd cli \u0026\u0026 go test ./internal/server -run 'TestProject' -count=1 passes with at least one test covering scoped route resolution.","labels":["phase:build","area:api","kind:implementation"],"parent":"hx-bed139d3"}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:76:{"id":"corpus-4f627e852b5a","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Migrate bead execution and worker smoke specs to fixture data","description":"## Context\n\nThe frontend e2e specs for beads, executions, and workers require backend data and currently fail when Playwright serves static preview output without the Go DDx server. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:108:{"id":"corpus-720b519f6e95","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed focused bead with small acceptance surface","title":"correctness: move semantic failures out of route feedback","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1313 and docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1371\\nCategory: correctness\\nSeverity: high\\nDescription: CONTRACT-003 says DDx reports semantic task outcomes through RecordRouteAttempt and that route-target-quality/task-failure contributes to route-target/runner reliability for future ranking. The clarified boundary is that agent does not track semantic success/failure directly. Agent only reports availability/network/protocol-level failure. Semantic task outcomes belong to the caller and may be contributed to the route-target catalog or catalog-derived power rating, not to agent's transient route feedback store.\\nSuggested fix: Remove route-target-quality/task-failure from RouteAttempt route-health semantics, redefine RecordRouteAttempt or a separate catalog-feedback mechanism for caller-provided semantic evidence, and document how that evidence affects catalog power ratings outside the agent dispatch loop.","acceptance":"CONTRACT-003 no longer states that semantic task failures directly demote route candidates in agent; docs distinguish upstream-service availability feedback from caller-owned semantic catalog feedback; failure classes exclude route-target-quality/task-failure from agent route-health penalties","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"bead":{"id":"agent-8460e36e","title":"correctness: move semantic failures out of route feedback","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1313 and docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1371\\nCategory: correctness\\nSeverity: high\\nDescription: CONTRACT-003 says DDx reports semantic task outcomes through RecordRouteAttempt and that route-target-quality/task-failure contributes to route-target/runner reliability for future ranking. The clarified boundary is that agent does not track semantic success/failure directly. Agent only reports availability/network/protocol-level failure. Semantic task outcomes belong to the caller and may be contributed to the route-target catalog or catalog-derived power rating, not to agent's transient route feedback store.\\nSuggested fix: Remove route-target-quality/task-failure from RouteAttempt route-health semantics, redefine RecordRouteAttempt or a separate catalog-feedback mechanism for caller-provided semantic evidence, and document how that evidence affects catalog power ratings outside the agent dispatch loop.","acceptance":"CONTRACT-003 no longer states that semantic task failures directly demote route candidates in agent; docs distinguish upstream-service availability feedback from caller-owned semantic catalog feedback; failure classes exclude route-target-quality/task-failure from agent route-health penalties","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"]}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:117:{"id":"corpus-7c1aedcf73aa","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"execute-loop: push_failed should NOT set a 1-year cooldown — race-with-remote is recoverable","description":"Today, when execute-bead lands locally but push fails (e.g. non-fast-forward because remote advanced), the loop sets execute-loop-retry-after to ONE YEAR in the future. This is wrong on multiple axes:\n\n1. Push race is fundamentally recoverable: pull/merge/push is the standard fix and takes seconds.\n2. A 1-year cooldown means the bead never re-runs in any reasonable working horizon.\n3. The work is already done locally — the bead is functionally complete; reopening it for a year is misleading.\n\nObserved in this session on ddx-98e6e9ef (routing-preflight-gate): worker landed commit 978131c6, push rejected because origin/main had advanced (687fb4ca + 4a6c6e8a pushed remotely between the local land and the push). Loop set retry_after=2027-04-29T03:43:59Z. Operator had to manually merge, push, and clear the cooldown.\n\nWhat is needed:\n\n1. push_failed should auto-pull/merge/retry-push at least once before giving up. The merge case where origin advanced with non-overlapping work is the common case in a multi-worker / multi-operator setup.\n\n2. If the auto-merge has conflicts that the loop cannot resolve, push_failed should set a SHORT cooldown (5-15 min), not 1 year. The operator just needs time to resolve the conflict.\n\n3. push_failed-with-conflicts should mark the bead as needing-human-review (a structured outcome similar to declined_needs_decomposition from ddx-fba752b9), not as a generic execution failure.\n\n4. The loop's cooldown duration should NEVER exceed some reasonable cap (e.g. 24h) for any outcome. Year-scale cooldowns mean 'never' and that should be a deliberate operator choice via 'ddx bead update --set execute-loop-retry-after=...', not an automatic loop decision.","acceptance":"1. push_failed flow attempts auto pull --rebase / merge / retry-push at least once before exiting with push_failed. 2. push_failed cooldown caps at 24h regardless of underlying error. 3. push_failed-with-conflicts (auto-merge fails) emits a structured outcome distinct from generic execution_failed; bead is parked for human review with the conflict context recorded as a structured event (kind:push-conflict). 4. Regression test: a fake git that returns non-fast-forward on first push, then fast-forward after pull/merge, exercises the new flow and asserts the bead lands cleanly without operator intervention. 5. Regression test: a fake git that produces an unresolvable merge conflict exercises the structured-outcome path and asserts cooldown \u003c= 24h. 6. CHANGELOG entry. 7. Update ddx-fba752b9's CHANGELOG section (or a new section) noting that the 24h cap applies to all loop-set cooldowns, not just push failures.","labels":["execute-loop","beads","quality-of-life"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-a458af7c","title":"execute-loop: push_failed should NOT set a 1-year cooldown — race-with-remote is recoverable","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"Today, when execute-bead lands locally but push fails (e.g. non-fast-forward because remote advanced), the loop sets execute-loop-retry-after to ONE YEAR in the future. Loop set retry_after=2027-04-29T03:43:59Z. Operator had to manually merge, push, and clear the cooldown.\n\nWhat is needed:\n\n1. push_failed should auto-pull/merge/retry-push at least once before giving up. The merge case where origin advanced with non-overlapping work is the common case in a multi-worker / multi-operator setup.\n\n2. The loop's cooldown duration should NEVER exceed some reasonable cap (e.g. 24h) for any outcome. Year-scale cooldowns mean 'never' and that should be a deliberate operator choice via 'ddx bead update --set execute-loop-retry-after=...', not an automatic loop decision.","acceptance":"1. push_failed flow attempts auto pull --rebase / merge / retry-push at least once before exiting with push_failed. 2. push_failed cooldown caps at 24h regardless of underlying error. 3. push_failed-with-conflicts (auto-merge fails) emits a structured outcome distinct from generic execution_failed; bead is parked for human review with the conflict context recorded as a structured event (kind:push-conflict). 4. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:139:{"id":"corpus-9725c274b8e3","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound","description":"The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Downstream consumer DDx ran into this mid-session on a production worker: ~8461 stuck-compaction events accumulated before the hosting process had to be killed.\n\n## Evidence\n\nDDx-side session log:\n/Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)\nParent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.\n\n## DDx-side workaround (defense in depth, does not fix the root cause)\n\nCommit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 in ddx repo (bead ddx-25a41f51) wraps the embedded agent call with two guards:\n1. compaction.start and compaction.end(no_compaction=true) events no longer pulse the caller's inactivity timer — only real-progress events (LLM calls, tool results, commits) extend the timeout.\n2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).\n\nThe workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.\n\n## Requested upstream fix\n\n- Compaction driver detects its own stuck state via the same heuristic (consecutive success=false/no_compaction=true events) and returns a structured error to the caller\n- Eliminates the need for the caller to interpret compaction events as activity signals\n- Exposes a clean API signal so consumers can decide whether to retry, escalate, or abort\n- Regression test seeds the stuck state and verifies the driver bails instead of looping\n\n## Scope\n\n- Bounded attempt counter inside the compaction loop (not just the caller)\n- Structured error: compaction.ErrStuck or similar\n- Integration test using a deterministic seed that reproduces the 33MB jsonl pattern\n\n## Relationship to DDx\n\nWhen this lands upstream, DDx can remove its circuit-breaker workaround and rely on the native error. Until then, DDx keeps both layers as belt-and-suspenders.","acceptance":"(1) Compaction loop bounded with a structural stop. (2) Native structured error signals stuck state to callers. (3) Regression test reproduces the stuck state via seeded read-only tool calls. (4) DDx repo can remove the caller-side circuit breaker once this upstream fix is consumed.","labels":["agent","kind:bug","area:compaction","severity:high","upstream-request"],"bead":{"id":"agent-e5228424","title":"bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Downstream consumer DDx ran into this mid-session on a production worker: ~8461 stuck-compaction events accumulated before the hosting process had to be killed.\n\n## Evidence\n\nDDx-side session log:\n/Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)\nParent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.\n\n## DDx-side workaround (defense in depth, does not fix the root cause)\n\nCommit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 in ddx repo (bead ddx-25a41f51) wraps the embedded agent call with two guards:\n1. compaction.start and compaction.end(no_compaction=true) events no longer pulse the caller's inactivity timer — only real-progress events (LLM calls, tool results, commits) extend the timeout.\n2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).\n\nThe workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.\n\n## Requested upstream fix\n\n- Compaction driver detects its own stuck state via the same heuristic (consecutive success=false/no_compaction=true events) and returns a structured error to the caller\n- Eliminates the need for the caller to interpret compaction events as activity signals\n- Exposes a clean API signal so consumers can decide whether to retry, escalate, or abort\n- Regression test seeds the stuck state and verifies the driver bails instead of looping\n\n## Scope\n\n- Bounded attempt counter inside the compaction loop (not just the caller)\n- Structured error: compaction.ErrStuck or similar\n- Integration test using a deterministic seed that reproduces the 33MB jsonl pattern\n\n## Relationship to DDx\n\nWhen this lands upstream, DDx can remove its circuit-breaker workaround and rely on the native error. Until then, DDx keeps both layers as belt-and-suspenders.","acceptance":"(1) Compaction loop bounded with a structural stop. (2) Native structured error signals stuck state to callers. (3) Regression test reproduces the stuck state via seeded read-only tool calls. (4) DDx repo can remove the caller-side circuit breaker once this upstream fix is consumed.","labels":["agent","kind:bug","area:compaction","severity:high","upstream-request"]}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:157:{"id":"corpus-ad8a89485d57","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Dashboard UI: live workers page with per-worker prompt, log, and utilization detail","description":"The server knows everything about running workers (state, runner, route-target, bead, attempts, prompt file, structured event log, token/cost/duration) via /api/agent/workers endpoints but the web UI has no live workers view. When an operator selects the eitri node they should land on (or reach in one click) an Agents page that lists every running worker from /api/agent/workers with bead-id, runner, route-target, state, elapsed, and latest status line. Clicking a row should open a worker detail page showing: (1) the rendered bead prompt from .ddx/executions/\u003cattempt\u003e/prompt.md, (2) the structured session log stream from .ddx/agent-logs/agent-loop-\u003csession\u003e.jsonl rendered via FormatSessionLogLines (so tool calls, turn counts, and timings are readable), (3) a session utilization panel with input/output/total tokens, cost-if-available, duration, attempts and successes/failures from the worker record, and (4) the base-rev and result-rev link once the worker has made progress. Polling at ~2s is fine for v1; no WebSocket required.\\n\\nBacking endpoints that already exist:\\n- GET /api/agent/workers -\u003e list\\n- GET /api/agent/workers/:id -\u003e detail\\n- GET /api/agent/workers/:id/log -\u003e stdout+stderr streams\\n\\nMissing backing endpoints (if not already there): GET /api/agent/workers/:id/prompt to serve prompt.md content without requiring the UI to know the execution bundle path; optionally /api/agent/workers/:id/events to serve the structured JSONL stream if /log doesn't already cover it.","acceptance":"Web UI route /nodes/:nodeId/agents (or /workers) lists all workers from /api/agent/workers with auto-refresh every ~2s. A worker row links to /nodes/:nodeId/agents/:workerId with three panels: prompt, log stream (structured events rendered with FormatSessionLogLines symbols), utilization (tokens/cost/duration/attempts). Both pages work against the alpha14 server without any additional backend work beyond optionally exposing the prompt content. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:211:{"id":"corpus-e3d55d44b044","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead","description":"There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Runner, WorkerRecord.Route-target, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.\n\nThis bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.\n\n## Required changes\n\n### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)\nRegister alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:\n  --json         emit raw JSON array\n  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C\n  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)\n\n### 2. Data sources (two paths)\nA. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.\nB. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:\n   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)\n   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, runner, route-target, started_at\n\n### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan\nFor each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:\n  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)\n  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)\n  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available\n  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields\n\n### 4. Table output (default)\nColumns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | RUNNER | ROUTE-TARGET | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS\n- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)\n- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)\n- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID\n\n### 5. Merge and deduplicate\nWhen both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.\n\n## Key files\n- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()\n- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)\n- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)","acceptance":"ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, RUNNER, ROUTE-TARGET, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. Server-submitted workers show state from WorkerManager. Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.","labels":["ux","execute-loop","observability","cli"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-9060c8b2","title":"Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:229:{"id":"corpus-f546b016a732","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed focused bead with small acceptance surface","title":"Implement deterministic script runner for orchestration stress testing","description":"The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every \"fake\" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. Neither produces actual commits that the orchestration layer can rebase, merge, or land.\n\nThe consequence: every test that claims to cover concurrency, serialization, or git orchestration uses mocked git and therefore cannot catch merge-race, rebase-conflict, or coordinator-serialization bugs. `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace` at execute_bead_orchestrator_test.go:228 is particularly misleading — it uses separate orchTestGitOps instances per worker so the shared-writer invariant is never exercised. The test passes because no shared state exists to race on.\n\n## What this bead builds\n\nA new deterministic `script` runner (cli/internal/agent/script.go, mirroring cli/internal/agent/virtual.go):\n\n- Registered alongside claude/codex/agent/virtual in cli/internal/agent/registry.go\n- Always available (no binary probe required)\n- Takes a directive file (passed via RunOptions.Route-target or a new RunOptions.ScriptFile field — either works)\n- Reads the directive file, executes each line against opts.WorkDir as real filesystem + git operations\n- Returns a real Result{ExitCode, Output} that the loop and orchestrator can observe\n\n## Directive grammar\n\nLine-based, comments start with `#`. Each directive performs a real side effect:\n\n```\nappend-line \u003cpath\u003e \u003ctext\u003e          # append text to file (create if missing)\ncreate-file \u003cpath\u003e \u003ccontent\u003e       # create or overwrite file\ndelete-file \u003cpath\u003e                 # rm -f path\nmodify-line \u003cpath\u003e \u003cregex\u003e \u003crepl\u003e  # sed-style in-place substitution\nrun \u003cshell-command\u003e                # exec in WorkDir with clean env\ncommit \u003cmessage\u003e                   # git add -A \u0026\u0026 git commit -m \u003cmessage\u003e\nset-exit \u003ccode\u003e                    # final exit code (default 0)\nno-op                              # no commits, no files, exit 0\nfail-during \u003cindex\u003e                # synthetic failure at the Nth directive\nsleep-ms \u003cn\u003e                       # deterministic delay (for scheduling tests)\n```\n\nDirectives run in order. Environment variables DDX_BEAD_ID, DDX_ATTEMPT_ID, DDX_WORKER_ID are interpolated into text arguments so directives can be parameterized. Example:\n\n```\nappend-line tests/smoke.txt \"hello from ${DDX_BEAD_ID}\"\ncommit \"smoke: ${DDX_BEAD_ID}\"\n```\n\n## Invocation\n\nFrom the CLI: `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=./testdata/append-smoke.txt --prompt=/dev/null`\n\nProgrammatically from a test:\n```go\nrunner := agent.NewRunner(agent.Config{Runner: \"script\"})\nresult, err := runner.Run(agent.RunOptions{\n    WorkDir: wtPath,\n    Route-target:   \"testdata/append-smoke.txt\",\n    Correlation: map[string]string{\"bead_id\": beadID, \"attempt_id\": attemptID},\n})\n```\n\n## Implementation notes\n\n- Mirror the dispatch pattern in cli/internal/agent/runner.go:81 where `virtual` is intercepted\n- Always-available in Discover() same as virtual + agent\n- No external dependencies beyond what the agent package already uses (os, os/exec for git)\n- Estimated size: ~200 lines of implementation + ~200 lines of tests\n- Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go must prove each directive actually performs its claimed side effect on a real temp git repo\n\n## What this enables\n\nEvery test case in beads B (integration tests) and C (server runWorker regression test) depends on this runner existing. Without it, the land-coordinator redesign ships behind whatever real claude happens to do on the day the test runs.","acceptance":"New file cli/internal/agent/script.go implements RunScript(opts) that reads a directive file, executes directives against opts.WorkDir using real filesystem and git operations, and returns a real Result. The runner is registered in cli/internal/agent/registry.go as `script` and marked always-available in Discover(). Directive grammar supports at minimum: append-line, create-file, delete-file, commit, set-exit, no-op, sleep-ms, fail-during. Environment variable interpolation supports ${DDX_BEAD_ID}, ${DDX_ATTEMPT_ID}, ${DDX_WORKER_ID}. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go cover: (1) append-line + commit produces a real commit with the expected content, (2) no-op produces zero commits and exit 0, (3) create-file + commit produces the file and a commit, (4) set-exit 1 makes the run return exit 1, (5) fail-during 2 executes directives 0 and 1 but errors on directive 2, (6) env var interpolation replaces ${DDX_BEAD_ID} with the value from Correlation, (7) invoking via `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=\u003cfile\u003e` works end-to-end with a real temp git repo. go test ./internal/agent -run TestScript completes in under 5 seconds.","labels":["area:agent","area:testing","kind:infrastructure","phase:build"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-025046ac","title":"Implement deterministic script runner for orchestration stress testing","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every \"fake\" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. Neither produces actual commits that the orchestration layer can rebase, merge, or land.\n\nThe consequence: every test that claims to cover concurrency, serialization, or git orchestration uses mocked git and therefore cannot catch merge-race, rebase-conflict, or coordinator-serialization bugs. `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace` at execute_bead_orchestrator_test.go:228 is particularly misleading — it uses separate orchTestGitOps instances per worker so the shared-writer invariant is never exercised. The test passes because no shared state exists to race on.\n\n## What this bead builds\n\nA new deterministic `script` runner (cli/internal/agent/script.go, mirroring cli/internal/agent/virtual.go):\n\n- Registered alongside claude/codex/agent/virtual in cli/internal/agent/registry.go\n- Always available (no binary probe required)\n- Takes a directive file (passed via RunOptions.Route-target or a new RunOptions.ScriptFile field — either works)\n- Reads the directive file, executes each line against opts.WorkDir as real filesystem + git operations\n- Returns a real Result{ExitCode, Output} that the loop and orchestrator can observe\n\n## Directive grammar\n\nLine-based, comments start with `#`. Each directive performs a real side effect:\n\n```\nappend-line \u003cpath\u003e \u003ctext\u003e          # append text to file (create if missing)\ncreate-file \u003cpath\u003e \u003ccontent\u003e       # create or overwrite file\ndelete-file \u003cpath\u003e                 # rm -f path\nmodify-line \u003cpath\u003e \u003cregex\u003e \u003crepl\u003e  # sed-style in-place substitution\nrun \u003cshell-command\u003e                # exec in WorkDir with clean env\ncommit \u003cmessage\u003e                   # git add -A \u0026\u0026 git commit -m \u003cmessage\u003e\nset-exit \u003ccode\u003e                    # final exit code (default 0)\nno-op                              # no commits, no files, exit 0\nfail-during \u003cindex\u003e                # synthetic failure at the Nth directive\nsleep-ms \u003cn\u003e                       # deterministic delay (for scheduling tests)\n```\n\nDirectives run in order. Environment variables DDX_BEAD_ID, DDX_ATTEMPT_ID, DDX_WORKER_ID are interpolated into text arguments so directives can be parameterized. Example:\n\n```\nappend-line tests/smoke.txt \"hello from ${DDX_BEAD_ID}\"\ncommit \"smoke: ${DDX_BEAD_ID}\"\n```\n\n## Invocation\n\nFrom the CLI: `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=./testdata/append-smoke.txt --prompt=/dev/null`\n\nProgrammatically from a test:\n```go\nrunner := agent.NewRunner(agent.Config{Runner: \"script\"})\nresult, err := runner.Run(agent.RunOptions{\n    WorkDir: wtPath,\n    Route-target:   \"testdata/append-smoke.txt\",\n    Correlation: map[string]string{\"bead_id\": beadID, \"attempt_id\": attemptID},\n})\n```\n\n## Implementation notes\n\n- Mirror the dispatch pattern in cli/internal/agent/runner.go:81 where `virtual` is intercepted\n- Always-available in Discover() same as virtual + agent\n- No external dependencies beyond what the agent package already uses (os, os/exec for git)\n- Estimated size: ~200 lines of implementation + ~200 lines of tests\n- Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go must prove each directive actually performs its claimed side effect on a real temp git repo\n\n## What this enables\n\nEvery test case in beads B (integration tests) and C (server runWorker regression test) depends on this runner existing. Without it, the land-coordinator redesign ships behind whatever real claude happens to do on the day the test runs.","acceptance":"New file cli/internal/agent/script.go implements RunScript(opts) that reads a directive file, executes directives against opts.WorkDir using real filesystem and git operations, and returns a real Result. The runner is registered in cli/internal/agent/registry.go as `script` and marked always-available in Discover(). Directive grammar supports at minimum: append-line, create-file, delete-file, commit, set-exit, no-op, sleep-ms, fail-during. Environment variable interpolation supports ${DDX_BEAD_ID}, ${DDX_ATTEMPT_ID}, ${DDX_WORKER_ID}. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go cover: (1) append-line + commit produces a real commit with the expected content, (2) no-op produces zero commits and exit 0, (3) create-file + commit produces the file and a commit, (4) set-exit 1 makes the run return exit 1, (5) fail-during 2 executes directives 0 and 1 but errors on directive 2, (6) env var interpolation replaces ${DDX_BEAD_ID} with the value from Correlation, (7) invoking via `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=\u003cfile\u003e` works end-to-end with a real temp git repo. go test ./internal/agent -run TestScript completes in under 5 seconds.","labels":["area:agent","area:testing","kind:infrastructure","phase:build"],"parent":"ddx-8746d8a6"}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:238:{"id":"corpus-011259e83fe4","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","description":"## Observed\n\nUser clicks \"Drain queue\" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/\u003cid\u003e/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.\n\nVerified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.\n\n```\n$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json\n{\n  \"id\": \"worker-20260422T205846-f23f\",\n  \"kind\": \"execute-loop\",\n  \"state\": \"running\",\n  \"project_root\": \"/Users/erik/Projects/ddx\",\n  ...\n}\n```\n\nSo the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.\n\n## Root cause\n\n`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:\n\n```go\nif projectID != \"\" \u0026\u0026 rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {\n    continue\n}\n```\n\n- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.\n- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.\n\nThese are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.\n\nThe global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `\"\"` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.\n\nNote: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nTwo options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.\n\n### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site\n\n```go\nexpectedPath := \"\"\nif projectID != \"\" {\n    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)\n    if !ok {\n        return nil // unknown project id → no workers\n    }\n    expectedPath = proj.Path\n}\n...\nif expectedPath != \"\" \u0026\u0026 rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {\n    continue\n}\n```\n\n### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record\n\nAdd `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).\n\nRecommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.\n\n## Scope note\n\nThis is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).\n- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.","acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.\n\n2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**\n   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).\n   - Start one worker for each project.\n   - `workersByProject(projectID: \u003cid-1\u003e)` returns exactly worker 1.\n   - `workersByProject(projectID: \u003cid-2\u003e)` returns exactly worker 2.\n   - `workers` (global) returns both.\n   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.\n\n3. **Regression test — backward compat.**\n   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.\n\n4. **End-to-end validation.**\n   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.\n   - Same test run against a second project asserts that project's Workers tab does not show project-1's worker.\n\n5. **No regression to unrelated surfaces.**\n   - Global `workers` query still returns all workers.\n   - Worker detail page still loads by worker id.\n   - Workers live-progress subscription still patches rows in place.\n\n6. **Cross-reference.**\n   - Note on `ddx-69789664` (Workers lifecycle controls): that bead's acceptance assumed Workers pane worked correctly. Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","bug","graphql"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-05b4cc9d","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"## Observed\n\nUser clicks \"Drain queue\" on the project home page. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:245:{"id":"corpus-42fe11ad31e3","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"three or more attempts without a closing commit","title":"work: ready epics are silently skipped by execution loop","description":"In the agent repo on 2026-04-20, `ddx bead ready --json` reported three ready open beads: two epics and one task. The task (`agent-05d81638`) was under retry cooldown until 2026-04-20T06:08:22Z. Running `ddx work --once` started a worker (`worker-20260420T004727-aff9`) that exited with 0 attempts and no log output. Only after running `ddx bead ready --execution --json` was it clear that the execution-ready queue was empty.\n\nThis is confusing operator behavior: `ready` says there is work, but `work` silently does nothing because epics are not execution-eligible. If epics are intentionally container-only, the default ready surface or the work-loop output should make that distinction explicit. If epics are supposed to be executable planning/decomposition targets, the work loop should handle them rather than dropping them.\n\nExpected behavior: when `ddx work` skips ready epics, the operator gets a clear reason and next action, e.g. \"0 execution-ready beads; skipped 2 epics because epics require child task decomposition; 1 task blocked by retry cooldown until \u003ctimestamp\u003e\". Alternatively, `ddx bead ready` should default to execution-ready or visibly mark non-execution-eligible epics so it does not imply `ddx work` can process them.","acceptance":"1. A regression test covers a tracker state with at least one open ready epic and no execution-eligible task beads; `ddx work --once` does not silently exit with an empty log/status.\n2. Existing queue/work tests pass.","labels":["area:beads","area:agent","kind:bug","wave:1","workstream:ddx-local"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-672cf2bf","title":"work: ready epics are silently skipped by execution loop","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"In the agent repo on 2026-04-20, `ddx bead ready --json` reported three ready open beads: two epics and one task. Running `ddx work --once` started a worker (`worker-20260420T004727-aff9`) that exited with 0 attempts and no log output. Only after running `ddx bead ready --execution --json` was it clear that the execution-ready queue was empty.\n\nThis is confusing operator behavior: `ready` says there is work, but `work` silently does nothing because epics are not execution-eligible. If epics are intentionally container-only, the default ready surface or the work-loop output should make that distinction explicit. If epics are supposed to be executable planning/decomposition targets, the work loop should handle them rather than dropping them.\n\nExpected behavior: when `ddx work` skips ready epics, the operator gets a clear reason and next action, e.g. \"0 execution-ready beads; skipped 2 epics because epics require child task decomposition; 1 task blocked by retry cooldown until \u003ctimestamp\u003e\". ./docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:9:The Efficacy page had a standalone "Compare" button that opened an empty modal asking the user to add "arms" (model + prompt pairs) from scratch. ./docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:15:- **Schema surface.** `schema.graphql:2499-2613` — `comparisonDispatch` and `ComparisonArmInput` already accept optional `harness` and `provider` alongside `model`+`prompt`. Pre-seeding from efficacy row tuples requires no schema change.
./docs/helix/02-design/efficacy-compare-redesign.md:24:The Efficacy page gains a leading checkbox column on the rollup table. Selecting 2+ rows enables a "Compare selected" header button that dispatches a comparison pre-seeded with the selected rows' `(harness, provider, model)` tuples. The user supplies only the prompt. ./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-030-axon-bead-backend.md:59:| D5 | **Wire transport**: GraphQL only. | All DDx↔Axon traffic is GraphQL — queries and mutations over HTTP, subscriptions over WebSocket. No gRPC, no REST, no MCP from the DDx side. The Go client is generated by `genqlient` from a pinned schema-introspection snapshot. Rationale: Axon's GraphQL surface already covers entity CRUD, links, traversal, transactions, schema, and subscriptions; DDx is already a GraphQL server internally (`cli/internal/server/graphql_*.go`), so DDx↔Axon is wire-symmetric; subscriptions unlock live UI updates for `ddx work` (Story 8) without polling; no protoc/codegen toolchain enters the DDx build. The per-call wire-overhead advantage of gRPC is immeasurable at DDx scale (low thousands of beads); if batch-transaction throughput later becomes painful for one specific path, gRPC can be added there without revisiting this decision. |

./docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-04-14-server-plan.md:42:- **Server topology** has evolved from a single-project server to a per-user host daemon: one `ddx server` process per machine per user, holding identity and a project registry in `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json` (FEAT-020). SD-019 specifies the multi-project routing, per-project isolation, and worker-pool model. FEAT-002 was updated to reflect this topology.
./docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-04-14-server-plan.md:43:- **Embedded-agent progress** is now fully specified in FEAT-006 with a structured progress event schema (phases, phase_seq, heartbeat), an SSE endpoint contract (`GET /api/projects/:project/workers/:id/progress`), and a worker state read model with `current_attempt`/`recent_phases`/`last_attempt`.
./docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-04-14-server-plan.md:44:- **Provider dashboard** is now specified in both FEAT-002 (requirements 26-27: `/api/providers` and `/api/providers/:harness`) and FEAT-008 (requirement 7: Provider/Harness Dashboard UI) with FEAT-014 governing field semantics, unknown-state rules, and the tooltip registry.
./docs/helix/06-iterate/alignment-reviews/AR-2026-04-14-server-plan.md:47:- **TP-002** was updated with six new test sections: TC-010 (project registry/scoped routing), TC-011 (host+user state/node identity), TC-012 (project isolation and concurrency), TC-013 (execute-loop worker lifecycle) — all PLANNED.

./website/content/features/_index.md:88:DDx talks to agents through a uniform harness interface. Swap Claude for Codex for a local model by changing configuration, not commands. The same `ddx agent run` invocation works across providers; cost, tokens, and latency are recorded the same way regardless of which harness answered.
./website/content/why/_index.md:49:Standardize the contract; let implementations compete underneath. Harnesses plug in via a prompt-in/output-out interface; routing is by endpoint and capability, not by hardcoded provider name; storage is plain files and standard git. Switching a model, a harness, or a host is a configuration change, not a migration.
